data.title: Merck Chooses Evolution Over Revolution - At Proposed Price, I'm Not Surprised
data.description:
Investment Overview

The last time I covered the New Jersey-based pharma giant Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) in a note for Seeking Alpha, I was discussing its ~$9.2bn buyout of Cidara Therapeutics, gaining access to Cidara's drug candidate CD388, a long-acting antiviral in development to treat broad-spectrum influenza prophylaxis.

In this note, I'll be covering news of a deal Merck has ultimately decided not to make - according to various media outlets, the Pharma has pulled out of discussions to acquire Revolution Medicines, Inc. (RVMD), having been unable to agree on a price for the deal, sources have suggested.

I gave Revolution stock a Buy rating back in September last year, citing the promise of its lead clinical candidate daraxonrasib and the pipeline of RAS(ON) inhibiting drugs - as the company writes in its Q3 2025 quarterly report/10Q filing:

Our RAS(ON) Inhibitors are based on our proprietary tri-complex technology platform, which enables a highly differentiated approach to inhibiting the active, GTP-bound form of RAS, which we refer to as RAS(ON). We are developing a portfolio of compounds that we believe were the first RAS(ON) Inhibitors to use this mechanism of action. We believe that direct inhibitors of RAS(ON) suppress cell growth and survival and are less susceptible to adaptive resistance mechanisms recognized for RAS inhibitors that target the inactive, GDP-bound form of RAS, which we refer to as RAS(OFF) inhibitors.

RAS proteins are involved in cell signaling processes, and RAS is most highly expressed in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma ("PDAC"). In September last year, Revolution stock surged on strong Phase 1 data for daraxonrasib in metastatic pancreatic cancer, and the drug is in a registrational (i.e., data from the study can be used as part of an FDA approval submission) study in second line ("2L") metastatic PDAC, as a monotherapy, and in first line ("1L") metastatic, either as monotherapy or in combo with gemcitabine nab-paclitaxel ("GnP"), and as an adjuvant. A Phase 3 study evaluating daraxonrasib in non-small cell lung cancer is also underway.

Rationale For Merck's Proposed Deal For Revolution

After my September Buy call, when shares traded at $46, Revolution stock kept climbing, reaching $80 per share at the beginning of this year, and then jumping to >$100 per share, as rumors began to circulate that it was in talks with Merck.

Last Friday, at market close, the stock was trading at ~$120 per share, which translates to a market cap of >$23bn, and sources had suggested that Merck might pay $28bn-$30bn to complete its acquisition of Revolution. Rumors that AbbVie (ABBV), a similarly sized "Big Pharma" concern, was also interested in a deal for Revolution were denied by AbbVie.

From Merck's perspective, the rationale for the deal is clear - its two biggest selling products, the cancer med Keytruda and HPV vaccine Gardasil, are expected to lose their patent protection around 2028.

In 2025, I'd estimate Keytruda will earn ~$31bn of revenues, and Gardasil ~$5.6bn (down from $8.5bn in 2024, owing to decreased demand in China). That represents ~57% of Merck's total expected revenues for 2025; hence, the company is under pressure to find new sources of revenues or face declining revenues after 2028, which would inevitably result in a valuation downgrade. Merck's current share price is $107, up >11% on a 12-month basis, >40% on a five-year basis, and >120% on a ten-year basis. Market cap is presently $268bn.

As we can see in the slide below - taken from a presentation Revolution gave at the JP Morgan (JPM) Healthcare Conference earlier this month - the company has two other assets in clinical development - elironrasib and zoldonrasib - besides daraxonrasib, targeting colorectal cancer and NSCLC besides PDAC, and two preclinical candidates.

Daraxonrasib is furthest advanced - in 2L metastatic PDAC, the drug has achieved a confirmed objective response rate ("ORR") of 35% and 29%, respectively, in patients with RAS mutant PDAC with a RAS G12X mutation (n=26) or any RAS mutation (n=38), with a disease control rate ("DCR") of 92% and 95%, respectively, a median progression-free survival ("mPFS") of 8.5 months and 8.1 months, and a median overall survival ("OS") of 13.1 months and 15.6 months.

These data - albeit from a fairly small number of patients - emphasise the potential superiority of Revolution's candidate over current standards of care, while in the first line, Revolution has reported an ORR of 47% (38 patients) and DCR of 89% after a median follow-up of 9.3 months.

Analysts have suggested that Daroxonrasib could achieve peak annual revenues of >$7bn, which, if true, arguably justifies a deal value of $28bn-$30bn-as we can see below, multiple data catalysts are arriving in 2026 that could open up a path to approval, likely in the second half of next year:

Revolution is also a cash-rich company, reporting $1.93bn of cash and equivalents as of the end of Q3, with funds raised from a deal struck with Royalty Pharma (RPRX), worth up to $2bn, in exchange for tiered royalties on net sales of daraxonrasib and potentially zoldonrasib also.

Why Merck's Interest in Revolution May Have Fizzled Out

Merck also presented at the JPM Healthcare earlier this month and, as expected, addressed the Keytruda loss of exclusivity ("LOE") and its strategy for offsetting lost revenues.

Management was bullish, noting that while consensus 2028 sales of Keytruda were estimated at ~$35bn (presumably the final year before LOE), its mid-2030's revenue opportunity from potential new growth drivers - i.e., its drug development pipeline and recently launched products - was >$70bn, up >$20bn in the past 12 months.

Naturally, actual revenues are worth a lot more than forecast revenues, as there is a tangible risk they will not be realized for one reason or another (underperformance in clinical studies, rejected for approval, underwhelming sales, rival launches superior drugs, etc.), but Merck goes so far as to name ten drugs it believes can earn ~70% of its $70bn revenue figure, as per the slide below:

Long-acting HIV therapy islatravir could be approved in April this year and potentially be used alongside Gilead Sciences (GILD) lenacapavir (expected to be a multibillion selling drug), while the anti-TROP antibody drug conjugate sacituzumab tirumotecan, a cancer med indicated for endometrial cancer and NSCLC, could be a >$5bn per annum product, it's been speculated.

Merck paid $10bn to acquire Verona Pharma and its chronic obstructive pulmonary disorder ("COPD") drug Ohtuvayre in July last year, which also has multi-billion-dollar peak revenue expectations (and is already approved), and Cidara's CD-388 is another asset with multi-billion-dollar revenue potential. Winrevair, indicated for pulmonary arterial hypertension, is yet another drug with similarly high revenue expectations.

All told, Merck sees a >$25bn long-term commercial opportunity in oncology, >$20bn in cardiovascular, ~$15bn in infectious disease, >$5bn in immunology, and >$5bn in ophthalmology—the pharma completed the buyout of Eyebio last year in a deal that could eventually cost $3bn.

As such, we might ask whether Merck insiders truly felt that paying >$25bn to acquire Revolution was necessary, given an already full pipeline addressing five different therapeutic modalities. I count at least ten oncology drugs in Merck's pipeline already.

During his presentation at JPM this month, Merck's CEO Robert Davis discussed the company's M&A deal in some detail, noting:

It always begins with what is the science? It always comes down to, is the conviction of my scientific teams there behind the opportunity? What is the unmet need that we're addressing? And if there is high conviction and excitement among my scientists, there's an unmet need we can address.

If you look from a dollar perspective, we've been in that -- looking in that up to $15 billion range has been kind of where we've been looking. But we've been very clear, we're willing to go larger than that, but we only will do so following the exact same logic and discipline.

So I would say we're multi tens of billions of dollars -- is that the way -- I've got to look at my CFO. I would spend more, she won't let me. It's just the way it goes. But in the multi tens of billions. I would look at it differently and say, we are not limited from a balance sheet. It's more where do we see strategic opportunity.

Mixed messages, perhaps, but we may conclude that a price tag of $28bn is at the very limit of what Merck may wish to spend on M&A, and would the outlay be justified?

My guess would be that Merck does believe in Revolution's technology and anticipates the approval of daraxonrasib in particular, but may have concluded that given key positive registrational data readouts are still ahead of Revolution, not already achieved, there remains a risk of the drug falling short on safety or efficacy, and in that scenario, the Pharma would be forced to write off a huge sum of money.

We may also look at the deal Revolution agreed to with Royalty Pharma and note that a sizeable chunk of any future revenues goes to Royalty, and also consider the fact that Revolution is sufficiently well funded to go it alone commercially.

Concluding Analysis: What Happens Now (And How Should We Think About the Investment Opportunity For Revolution And Merck?)

In my view, Merck's proposed acquisition of Revolution feels like a deal that simply does not need to happen. Merck has a packed pipeline of oncology drugs, and thanks to internal R&D and strategic M&A already completed, it is strong in fields such as HIV, cardiovascular, immunology, and virology, etc., not to mention its >$3bn per annum hospital acute care business and >$6bn per annum animal health business.

Furthermore, the recent launch of Keytruda Qlex - a subcutaneous form of its most successful drug - could further offset losses from the patent expiry of the original Keytruda.

With its Verona, Cidara, and Eyebio deals, Merck has already committed >$20bn to M&A in the past year or so, which makes me think the Pharma may have viewed Revolution as a "nice-to-have" acquisition, rather than a "must-have."

Merck has forecast revenues of ~$64.5bn - $65bn in 2025, with GAAP earnings per share ("EPS") of $8.93 - $8.98, a forward price-to-sales ratio of ~4x, and a forward price-to-earnings ("P/E") of ~12x. These are healthy enough ratios for me to upgrade my rating on Merck stock to a Buy, as, although the Keytruda and Gardasil patent expiries are problematic for the company, and I'd expect a "trough" year in 2028/2029 (revenues falling annually), there is plenty of evidence to suggest the company can rapidly return to growth next decade, with a suite of new multi-billion dollar revenue assets, and with a balance sheet apparently not an impediment to making more M&A deals if necessary.

For Revolution, I feel that a market cap of >$18bn is high for a company with no approved products. Of course, that is likely to change if, as seems likely based on data readouts to date, daraxonrasib makes it to market, followed by other assets, but unless M&A speculation is revived, I'd anticipate the share prices' ongoing decline, at least in the short term.

Data catalysts due in 2026 may offset losses, but my feeling is the market may have gotten slightly ahead of itself in its pricing of Revolution, and the end of 2026 may be a better time to take stock of progress and potentially buy into the RAS-targeting story at a slightly deflated price. If I were CEO of Revolution, I'd consider dropping my price and making a deal with Merck at the ~$20bn mark, but as much as the biotech industry is desperate to celebrate a first major M&A deal of 2026, I suspect that may be unlikely.

data.title: KeyCorp: Strong NII, $1.2B Stock Buyback, Not Cheap
data.description:
KeyCorp (KEY) delivered a strong performance last week when the regional banking platform beat top and bottom line estimates amid a strong performance in the core lending business. KeyCorp benefits from a robust lending environment that is driving incremental credit demand, especially in the commercial sector, and reported a double-digit year-over-year increase in its net interest income. The lender also significantly increased the size of its commercial loan portfolio, which could continue to grow this year on the back of more federal fund rate cuts.

Previous rating

I rated shares of KeyCorp a hold in my last work—Unattractive Risk Profile—because the regional lender was almost certain to see lower interest rates and, as a result, a deterioration in its net interest income in a falling-rate world. However, I was expecting a more aggressive stance from the Federal Reserve with respect to rate cuts, which ultimately did not materialize. As a result, KeyCorp generated impressive growth in its net interest income and is guiding for $1.2B in stock buybacks in 2026. Considering all the pros and cons of an investment in KeyCorp, I feel it is most appropriate to rate shares of KeyCorp a hold.

KeyCorp delivers strong NII performance, resulting in double-beat

The regional lender beat top and bottom line estimates for the fourth fiscal quarter on January 20, 2026: KeyCorp reported $0.41 per share in non-GAAP earnings, beating the consensus estimate by $0.02 per share. The top line, driven mainly by net interest income gains, was published at $2.01B, outmatching the consensus estimate by $36M.

KeyCorp, like so many other Wall Street and regional banks, benefited from a favorable backdrop in the lending segment in the fourth quarter: the bank generated 12% year-over-year growth in revenue, which was published at $2.0B, with its net interest income surging almost at twice the rate as its non-interest income. KeyCorp also maintained high credit quality in the last quarter, which indicates to me that the regional bank is well-positioned for growth in the core business in 2026. KeyCorp's loan loss provisions were only $108M in the fourth quarter, up only $1M compared to Q3'25.

KeyCorp’s net interest income surged 15% year-over-year in the fourth quarter to $1.2B, with the bank's net interest margin seeing continual expansion in the last four quarters. The net interest margin as of the end of Q4’25 stood at 2.82% (+0.41 PP Y/Y), and the uptick was primarily driven by the regional lender reinvesting proceeds from maturing low-yielding securities and assets into higher-yielding loans and investments. Further, deposit costs declined, allowing the bank to pay less interest to clients that hold interest-bearing accounts on its lending platform.

The regional bank is still very much able to grow its loans, which is due to falling interest rates. The Federal Reserve cut federal fund rates by 25 basis points at the end of December to a new range of 3.50-3.75%, which is stimulating credit demand. What also benefits the outlook for KeyCorp's expansion in the loan portfolio is that the U.S. economy is growing rapidly, highlighting a strong consumer and business spending backdrop that could aid the bank's growth in 2026.

KeyCorp's average loans in the fourth quarter stood at $106.3B, showing 1.5% year-over-year growth. It was also the third consecutive quarter of sequential growth, driven by commercial loans. The total number of outstanding average commercial loans reached $76.0B, showing 5.4% year-over-year growth.

KeyCorp’s valuation and capital returns

KeyCorp is a leading regional mid-size banking platform that has seen steady growth in recent years in terms of deposits and loans. Shares of KeyCorp are currently trading at a 30% premium to book value, which compares to higher premiums for other regional banking franchises that also reported strong NII growth tailwinds in the last quarter.

U.S. Bancorp (USB) and PNC Financial (PNC) are trading at price-to-book ratios of 1.48X and 1.46X, while Truist Financial (TFC) is currently priced at the lowest P/B ratio in the regional banking industry group of 1.03X. Shares of KeyCorp are also trading above the bank's longer-term, 3-year average price-to-book ratio of 1.12X, which may be seen as an indication of slight overvaluation.

In my last work on KeyCorp, I calculated a fair value of $18.50 per share, based off of the bank's historical P/B ratio of 1.15X. Since KeyCorp's book value per share increased ~15% Y/Y to $16.27, I am raising my fair value estimate, using the same multiplier, to $18.71. Since shares currently trade a little higher than $21 per share, I consider KeyCorp to be slightly more than fully valued.

I aggressively recommended KeyCorp in early 2023 for its capital return potential and high dividend yield that resulted from the crisis playing out in the regional banking market in April 2023. The SVB-related sell-off in March 2023 created massive bargains in the industry, which I urged investors to exploit. I changed my rating from buy to hold in December 2023 due to the regional banking sector recovering from the crisis, leading to much lower dividend yields.

Currently, investors can earn a near-4% yield with KeyCorp, which positions the regional lender right at the top of the yield ranking: it is only superseded by Truist, which pays a yield of 4.2%. Further, KeyCorp has said that it plans to buy back more shares in 2026. According to the lender's initial guidance, KeyCorp seeks to return $1.2B+ in 2026 in the form of stock buybacks, which could provide crucial support for the share price.

Risks with KeyCorp

The biggest risk for KeyCorp and other regional lenders with large loan portfolios relates to additional federal fund rate cuts in 2026. The Federal Reserve is almost certainly going to lower interest rates at least one more time in 2026, which exposes KeyCorp to net interest income pressure. What would change my rating to sell is if the bank were to see a serious slowdown in its NII growth (to below 5% Y/Y) and lower credit quality, leading to a rise in loan loss provisions.

Final thoughts

KeyCorp submitted a robust earnings report card for the fourth fiscal quarter last week as the lender benefited from double-digit year-over-year revenue growth, driven chiefly by net interest income. The regional bank managed to maintain high balance sheet quality—it published a low amount of credit loss provisions for the fourth quarter—and guided for $1.2B in stock buybacks in 2026.

I like KeyCorp’s growth potential in the core commercial lending market, which has emerged as a major driver of net interest income growth for KeyCorp. While shares are not as expensive as those of PNC Financial and U.S. Bancorp, I see limited upside potential for the bank given its historical valuation average. That said, however, KeyCorp is still an attractive capital return play, offering investors a dividend of ~4% as well as stock buyback potential.

data.title: 7 Things To Consider If The U.S. Government Shuts Down
data.description:
Last September, investors had five things to consider ahead of the U.S. government shutdown. Last week, Axios reported that the House passed legislation that would prevent another shutdown. Unfortunately, on January 25, 2026, the odds of a shutdown abruptly shifted for the worse: the odds of a government shutdown by January 31 increased to 79%. This is up from 11.5% last Friday. At the time of submitting this article, Polymarket had an over 80% chance.

Why did the odds increase, and what should investors consider this time around? Readers benefit from observing what happened to the U.S. economy, economic reports, and the stock market during last year’s shutdown.

1/ What Happened?

Senate Democrats vowed to block the funding package worth $1.2 trillion. It included funds for the Department of Homeland Security. Funding to ICE, or Immigration and Customs Enforcement, is the highest-funded U.S. law enforcement agency. Over the weekend, a Federal agent shot and killed a 37-year-old Minneapolis man, who was an intensive care unit (“ICU”) nurse. Remembered as a “kind-hearted soul,” discussions on Reddit, Inc. (RDDT) about what happened received 707 comments and 74,400 upvotes. Readers may search the Internet to view two video captures of the events that took place.

As Seeking Alpha News reported, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer said on Saturday that “Senate Democrats will not provide the votes to proceed to the appropriations bill if the DHS funding bill is included.” Republicans have a 53-47 majority and need 60 votes for the bill to pass.

2/ Missing CPI Data

Investors might dismiss both the consumer price index and the non-farm payroll reports. In the November 2025 data set (table A here), the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported the CPI for just three items: gasoline (all types), new vehicles, and used cars and trucks. Despite the report providing readers with a limited view on inflation, the S&P 500 (SP500, IVV, SPY) trended higher between September and December.

3/ Delayed Non-farm Payroll Reports

Skepticism for the NFP reports accelerated after President Trump fired the BLS commissioner. The agency reported that hirings in July slowed. Additionally, it revised the June 2025 employment down to 14,000, compared to the previously reported figure of 147,000. In July, the NFP was 73,000.

Skepticism peaked after the BLS published the Current Employment Statistics (“CES”) national benchmark revision to total nonfarm employment for March 2025 at -911,000.

If the government shuts down again, investors might need to track corporate news releases on job cuts more closely. For example, last October, the media speculated that Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) would cut 30,000 jobs. The company cut around 14,000 white-collar jobs, half of what Reuters first reported. However, sources said that the e-commerce giant would top up the 30,000 job cuts by laying off another 16,000 corporate workers.

Layoffs.fyi provides data on job losses in the technology sector and the federal government. Still, readers may refer to that site at any time instead of relying on it in the absence of NFP reports.

4/ GDP Cost of Government 2025 Shutdown

On October 29, 2025, the Congressional Budget Office estimated that annualized real GDP growth would fall by 1.0% to 2.0% for the affected quarter. Since the government shutdown ended up lasting six weeks, over 1.25 million federal employees missed paychecks. That resulted in around $16 billion in direct lost wages. It interrupted SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program). The suspension of federal housing loans and permits delayed projects.

Readers may compare the fourth-quarter GDP with the recently revised third quarter report. The Bureau of Economic Analysis raised the third quarter GDP figure to 4.4% on January 22.

Between September and late November 2025, shares of D.R. Horton, Inc. (DHI) and Lennar Corporation (LEN) slumped toward a 52-week low. Their share prices rebounded this month, but the rally is fading. Markets are likely pricing in the risk of a slowdown in residential construction, due partly to the potential shutdown.

5/ Gold, Silver, and Copper Prices

The government’s inability to pass its funding bill might weaken the U.S. dollar (UUP, DXY). The UUP ETF lost 2.52% in the last week, trading at levels not seen since last October and during the last shutdown. Investors, and more likely central banks, are accumulating gold (GLD), silver (SLV), and copper (COPX, ICOP) instead. To achieve a store of value that preserves purchasing power, prices in those metals rose throughout last year and accelerated this month.

Gold topped $5,000 per ounce on January 25 and traded at $5,100.69 (XAUUSD:CUR) at the time of writing this article. A potential shutdown might push gold prices to even higher levels, as the exodus in the U.S. currency continues.

6/ Treasury Bonds and Japan’s Yields

Long-term treasury bond prices peaked last October and fell steadily throughout the last lockdown. Investors holding the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) appear nervous, as yields risk rising from here. This pattern suggests that TLT stock, along with the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF), might slump again if the government shuts down.

This time is especially risky for government bonds. In the far east, Japan’s 2-year, 10-year, and 40-year bond yields are trading at highs not seen in decades. For example, Japan’s 10-year government bond yield topped 2.35%. Yields for the other bond maturities are at levels not seen since 1994 – 1999. The higher Japan’s bond yields rise, the more it pressures investors to unwind their carry trade. Investors might sell U.S. assets, which include stocks and bonds.

7/ Unaffected Sectors

Consistent with my earlier bullish ratings, the defense and aerospace sector could potentially outperform the markets. “Geopolitical conflict” is the likely 2026 buzzword. The U.S. threat against Greenland, protests in Iran, and Ukraine’s defense against Russia are ongoing conflicts. The signing of military contracts with RTX Corporation (RTX), General Dynamics Corporation (GD), and Northrop Grumman Corporation (NOC) will continue despite a government shutdown.

Sorted by their Seeking Alpha Quant Rating, L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX), General Dynamics, and Leidos Holdings, Inc. (LDOS) have a score of around 4.0/5.0 or higher.

My watchlist of companies in this sector is Ondas Inc. (ONDS), Parker-Hannifin Corporation (PH), Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (RCAT), Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT), Textron Inc. (TXT), Honeywell International Inc. (HON), Parsons Corporation (PSN), and AeroVironment, Inc. (AVAV).

Your Takeaway

ETF investors are unlikely to worry about a government shutdown hurting the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI), Nasdaq (QQQ), and S&P 500. They are all at all-time highs, even though the rising M2 money supply contributes to their performance.

Markets might not react at first if the government shuts down. However, a prolonged one might prove problematic for stock markets. It would delay the BLS from reporting inflation and job data. That limits the Federal Reserve from making a more informed decision on monetary policy. For now, investors may focus on the Fed’s upcoming decision this week.

data.title: What If Europe Dumped Its Treasuries?
data.description:
Many market commentators and conspiracy theorists are postulating that Europe might dump its rather significant holdings of US Treasuries as revenge for the US taking over Greenland.

The chart below shows the number of Treasuries held by foreign countries to the nearest reporting period at the US Treasury

Europe has about $2 Trillion. These Treasuries came into being as a result of Europes export income from international trade with America. It represents the dollarized monetization of decades of imports from Europe.

The Europeans have sent real goods and services to America and are happy to save the income as dollars.

The thousands of businesses that export to America all have an account with a US bank that in turn has an account at the Federal Reserve Bank [Fed]. In these American bank accounts, the dollars accumulate as goods and services from Europe are bought and paid for by domestic Americans. The dollars move from a domestic American's bank account to a US Bank account in the name of a European entity. The Fed groups the bank accounts under a sub-account for Europe.

In theory, the European entities could spend those US dollars and buy anything offered for sale in US dollars. The dollars could re-enter the US domestic economy in return for US goods and services or assets.

The point to remember is that the dollars never leave the Fed's spreadsheet. All that happens is that they change ownership in exchange for goods and services. The Fed marks accounts up and down to track the dollar's movement.

US law prevents the Europeans and other foreigners from buying things for sale in US dollars and gives them little choice but to put their excess dollar holdings into a savings account and earn some interest. Foreigners are encouraged to buy US Treasuries (that accrue a coupon payment like a term deposit savings account) with their surplus dollars rather than keep the dollars in their checking account earning no interest.

Since 1960 the United States has drawn on world resources through a novel monetary process: by running balance-of-payments deficits that it refuses to settle in gold, it has obliged foreign governments to invest their surplus dollar holdings in Treasury bills, that is, to relend their dollar inflows to the U.S. Treasury.

(Hudson, Michael. Global Fracture: The New International Economic Order (p. 17). Pluto Press. Kindle Edition).

At the moment the European export earnings are in a savings account known as a treasury instead of dollars in a checking account earning nothing. If they move their dollars from savings to checking, they get no interest earnings on their dollars, just like you would at your bank. The primary dealer bank that holds their US bank account for them would then have excess reserves and buy treasuries with them, and so the interest income would go to the primary dealer bank where the Europeans have their bank account. The interest income would go into the private domestic sector via the interest on excess reserves or the interest on treasury deposits bank income channel and is a positive for the private domestic sector as it stops becoming external income and instead becomes private sector domestic income.

No sensible country with knowledge of reserve banking would choose to hold their dollar export earnings in cash rather than interest-earning Treasuries. Cash is, in effect, a zero-coupon bond.

There would be no sudden interest rate change. The Fed's mandate is to maintain its Federal Funds target rate [FFR] by buying and selling Treasury bonds. If the Europeans exited Treasuries, the excess liquidity would be immediately mopped up with a bond sale to whichever bank held the excess cash. If the Fed did not do this, the excess bank reserves sitting in the banking system would push the interest rate down below target.

The lowest the interest rate would fall to is the floor set by the interest on reserve balances, and this is presently 3.65%.

There would be no interest rate implications as the number of dollars and treasuries on the issue remain the same. All that happens is a portfolio swap - paper assets for cash.

The problem comes if the Europeans decide to sell their Treasuries, take their cash, and then exchange their cash for another currency. Then the USD could depreciate in international markets

Now Europe says, “We don’t want dollars; we want, say, euros or a basket.”

They go to the FX market and sell $2T for other currencies.

Transaction:

  • Europe:

    • –$2T USD deposits

    • +€ (or other currency) deposits

  • FX counter parties (rest of world):

    • –€ (or other currency) deposits

    • +$2T USD deposits

Dollars do not leave the U.S. system; they remain as liabilities of U.S. banks/the Fed, just now held by different owners (whoever bought the dollars from Europe).

FX effect:

  • If the flow is large and one‑sided, the USD depreciates against the target currencies until:

    • Some agents are willing to hold the extra $2T, or

    • Policy (Fed, other central banks) leans against the move.

The adjustment mechanism is the exchange rate, not “running out of dollars.”

Imports might become more expensive, giving more market share to domestic alternatives, and as domestic production expands, prices fall again. An adjustment measured in months rather than years.

The most likely thing to happen from European, or any other primary holder of US Treasuries, exiting their Treasury holdings would be a brief market panic brought about by news headlines informing the public of the event, the public drawing the wrong conclusions from it, and adding to the fear and misinformation.

So it is not going to happen, and if it does, it does not matter.

The worst thing that can happen is that the rest of the world decides to no longer send real goods and services to the US in exchange for dollar credits on the Fed's computer. Then we will have to make them ourselves, and this would involve resource usage, local pollution, and health and safety issues. Better, these externalities take place somewhere else.

So one can comfortably buy US Treasuries in the knowledge that they remain a risk-free income-producing asset (TLT), (TBT), or (IEF) in the largest and most liquid bond market in the world.

data.title: IAUI: Turning Gold Into Cash Flow With Some Tradeoffs
data.description:
Overview

With gold rallying to an all-time high, I thought it made sense to take a look at the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI). NEOS has maintained an excellent track record for its covered call ETFs, and the assumption is that they will be able to implement the same strategies against a portfolio of gold-focused assets. However, the fund is still relatively new, with an inception dating back to June 2025. Therefore, the performance window is fairly limited, and we've only seen how the fund performs through positive market momentum.

Looking at the performance since the fund's inception, we can see that IAUI's share price has increased by 21.5%. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up above 32.1% over the same time frame. IAUI offers investors an estimated annual distribution rate of 12.5% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. Additionally, the fund puts an emphasis on issuing distributions in a tax-efficient manner, which is great for investors who want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Despite the fund's strength so far, I believe there are certain tradeoffs and risks that investors should consider. The most straightforward tradeoff is that investors choosing to maintain a position in IAUI for the income potential will likely underperform more traditional gold-focused ETFs if this rally continues. Additionally, there's always the chance that IAUI's payouts will decline if the gold rally shifts momentum and values fall. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that IAUI implements to generate its amplified income levels.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund overview, IAUI has total net assets of $338.6M and has an expense ratio of 0.78%. While this expense ratio may be higher than traditional ETFs, it may be worth it considering the active management and the higher dividend yield offered by the fund. The fund's primary goal is to provide a high monthly income from a call option strategy that's implemented against gold-focused assets. Unlike other high-yield alternatives, IAUI is unique because it offers direct exposure to these securities, rather than initiating a synthetic approach.

When looking at the holdings of the fund, we can see that IAUI is most heavily allocated to U.S. Treasuries at 69.38% of assets. This helps the fund maintain a healthy level of liquidity through changing market conditions while also allowing IAUI to collect interest. This is followed by an allocation to the Goldman Sachs Physical Gold ETF (AAAU) at 24.45% of assets, which is how IAUI can directly participate in the momentum of gold. The remainder of the fund's assets is allocated across different active option positions, with varying expiration dates. These allow the fund to harness the volatility of the sector and capture option premiums to support the elevated distributions. The active options also have different strike prices as follows:

  • GLD 04/17/2026 - $395C.
  • GLD 04/17/2026 - $395P.
  • GLD 02/20/2026 - $418C.
  • GLD 02/20/2026 - $408C.

Lastly, I like the fact that IAUI utilizes OTM (out-of-the-money) calls as part of its strategy because this allows for a greater level of price appreciation to be captured. However, the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) currently trades around ~$467 per share, which means that IAUI's active options are ITM (in-the-money). While the goal is to create income from option premiums, the current positioning highlights one of the risks. With gold rallying to all-time highs, the short call options at the $408 and $418 strike prices, IAUI is unable to participate in any price appreciation above these levels. So while IAUI is capturing option premiums, the fund is missing out on a lot of upside growth.

Gold Outlook and Performance Risks

Gold has now crossed $5,000 per ounce for the first time in history, and many investors are excited to get in on the action. So will gold continue to rise throughout 2026? Well, I believe that there are several indicators to watch as they can be a great signal for which direction gold will move. In short, I believe these are three of the most important indicators:

  1. If central banks continue to buy gold.
  2. If the U.S. debt continues to rise.
  3. If global conflict/uncertainty drags on, such as the latest Greenland drama.

So, although gold now sits at its highest value ever, these are still compelling catalysts that can take it higher. However, these potential catalysts also highlight the single biggest risk for IAUI: underperformance over a longer holding period. Although the performance history is short, we can already see how IAUI underperforms against GLD and AAAU since its inception. IAUI's total return sits slightly above 32.1%, while AAAU and GLD both have total returns above 48% over the same time frame. This demonstrates that IAUI's higher dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to a higher total return over time.

This stunted performance can be directly attributed to the capped upside of the option writing strategy. So if you're an investor who believes gold will continue to rise over time, it would make more sense to directly own one of these traditional gold-tracking ETFs instead. Based on the underlying strategy of IAUI, I believe the fund's outlook can be summarized by the following scenarios:

  1. Gold Rises To $5,500 per ounce = Since IAUI's upside is limited, the fund will continue to underperform GLD and AAAU. IAUI will effectively sacrifice the upside growth to provide high distributions. The fund's distributions are likely to remain high, but this won't help the performance keep up with standard gold ETFs. Although IAUI will underperform, this is still a solid outcome for the fund.
  2. Gold momentum cools and hovers around $5,000 per ounce = This would be the absolutely best outcome for IAUI because the fund's share price is likely to remain within a tight range. However, the distributions can cause IAUI to outperform standard gold ETFs since AAAU and GLD would effectively return 0%. Investors would collect their monthly distribution while their invested capital stays intact.
  3. Gold reverses to $4,500 per ounce = Global tensions may ease, the U.S. dollar may strengthen, and this may trigger a sell-off. IAUI's upside growth is capped, but the downside risk is not. IAUI will also decline in value, but the distributions may help soften the declines compared to standard ETFs like AAAU and GLD. So you may lose less money than investors directly holding physical gold ETFs, but you will still see your invested capital decline in IAUI.

So in my opinion, IAUI would be best utilized as an accompanying fund for investors who have already ridden the positive gold momentum upward and are now becoming cautious about the potential of a pullback. IAUI can be a solid way to generate amplified income from this asset class, while it also offers a slight buffer against a pullback from the highs.

Dividend Utility

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.6092 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.5% at this time. Since the fund's operating history is so short, we only have a few months to measure the payout stability. IAUI has done a great job at keeping payouts within a consistent range, but I remain cautious about the sustainability if momentum shifts. Since gold has continually risen, IAUI has been able to secure higher option premiums and fuel these large payouts.

I suspect that the payout amounts will decline if gold pulls back from its current all-time highs. As the fund's net assets decline, the fund will have less capital that it can deploy into its option strategy, which translates to a reduced income potential. If IAUI chooses not to reduce its distributions, this can also translate to the underlying NAV declining over time. Another piece of the distribution is the higher volatility environment right now. If volatility fades, so will the income generation within the fund. However, this is theoretical since we haven't actually experienced a shift in IAUI's momentum yet.

These potential risks aside, one of the largest benefits is IAUI's emphasis on tax efficiency. According to the latest section 19(a) notice, 90% of the distributions paid YTD were classified as return of capital. This is favorable for investors because returns of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. So if you buy IAUI at $60 per share and it issues return of capital distributions totaling $7 per share, your cost basis at the end of the year would be reduced to $53 per share, and you would only pay taxes on the capital gain, assuming there is one.

However, tax-efficient distributions don't necessarily mean tax-free. As we can see, 10% of the distributions paid YTD were still classified as net investment income, which has the highest tax burden for investors. Since the year just started, the makeup of how distributions are paid can significantly shift by year-end, which is something to keep in mind for investors who want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that IAUI can be an attractive buy at these highs. If gold continues its positive growth momentum, IAUI will be able to participate in this growth with some restraints. Conversely, IAUI can still be attractive if gold shifts momentum and declines, since the distributions can act as a buffer to losses. However, the fund is still new, so we admittedly have no reference point for how the fund may perform through a shifting gold cycle. IAUI has maintained an attractive level of consistency with its distribution amounts, which can be great for investors who are looking to collect reliable dividends. Additionally, the fund prioritizes tax-efficient distributions, which is ideal for investors looking to minimize their overall tax liability. The most important thing to consider is that IAUI will underperform standard gold-focused ETFs if the rally continues.

data.title: Bank of Hawaii: Another Quarter Of Margin-Fueled Growth
data.description:
While it has largely tracked the fortunes of the wider regional banking space, Bank of Hawaii Corporation (BOH) has nevertheless been a decent performer of late. Shares of the Aloha State's top deposit gatherer have returned a respectable 13% since my last update in October, with the company benefiting from a rising tide of improving industry-wide sentiment and a positive reaction to its Q4 2025 results.

Rating it "Buy" in my last piece, I principally liked BOH's recovery-driven growth prospects. The short story: BOH’s net interest margin is steadily rebuilding, and this is driving strong earnings growth for the firm. Alongside moderating losses in its securities portfolio, this is also helping to reflate BOH’s capital cushion, opening the door to a meaningful return to share repurchases. BOH just reported its quarterly results, making now a good time to review this thesis. I remain bullish, with 2026 shaping up to be another good year for the bank.

Strong Margin-Driven Growth

Last quarter saw BOH deliver another period of strong growth, with its net income rising 56% year-on-year (14% sequentially) to $60.9 million. While the annual comp benefited slightly from lower credit charges, this growth is mainly due to higher pre-provision net revenue, as shown below.

Since BOH's interest-earning assets have remained flat, this performance is really being driven by a wider net interest margin. BOH's NIM was 2.61% last quarter, up 42 basis points from a year ago and 15 basis points from my last piece. After bottoming out in Q1 2024, the bank has now recorded seven straight quarters of sequential NIM expansion.

Cheaper funding and fixed-asset repricing continue to power this trend. On the liability side, BOH has been able to pass on some of the Fed's recent rate cuts to its depositors. The bank was paying 1.94% on its interest-bearing deposits last quarter, a reduction of 20 basis points from the prior quarter. Supplementing this, BOH saw a nice uptick in non-interest-bearing balances last quarter, driving a positive mix effect.

On the asset side, BOH is benefiting from the repricing of its $7.7 billion bond portfolio. BOH's securities account for 35% of its interest-earning assets, a higher share than the average bank. As a result, trends here are having an outsized impact on its financials.

Reflecting the fact that these bonds were mostly purchased before interest rates rose, the blended yield was still only 2.70% last quarter. Clearly, there is still a lot of repricing potential to exploit. As older securities mature, management can simply redeploy the proceeds at prevailing market rates, driving an uptick in interest income. That’s precisely what is happening: BOH generated $52 million in interest income from its securities last quarter, up 6% from Q3. Over the same period, the blended yield rose 11 basis points.

To be clear, lower interest rates are pressuring BOH's average loan yield, which fell 5 basis points from the prior quarter. However, due to its fixed-asset repricing, the bank is only losing a small amount on its overall asset yield, which fell just 1 basis point sequentially last quarter.

As I have said in other pieces, there are two attractive features of NIM-driven growth. The first is that there aren’t really any incremental operating costs associated with a higher interest margin, so this income largely drops to the bottom line. This is precisely what BOH is seeing, with its earnings growth comfortably outpacing its revenue.

The second is that a bank doesn’t need to retain more capital against a wider interest margin, which it may do if its revenue growth were coming from higher loan balances. This creates a nice tailwind for profitability metrics. Again, we can see this in BOH’s recent results. Last quarter's net income mapped to a return on equity of 15.03%, 144 basis points higher than the year-ago period and 473 basis points better than Q3.

Asset Quality Remains Pristine

While BOH continues to enjoy the benefits of a higher net interest margin, asset quality also remains in great shape. As I have set out before, this has historically been a strong suit of the bank. Real estate collateral backs 80% of its lending, and with a weighted loan-to-value ratio of just 51%, this has resulted in a much lower loss rate compared to the industry.

With the above in mind, last quarter's results don't offer any cause for concern. On the contrary, BOH's net charge-off rate clocked in at a lowly 0.12%, while just 0.10% of its assets held non-performing status.

More To Come In 2026

BOH's net interest margin should continue to expand in 2026. Indeed, despite rising strongly from its early-2024 low, BOH's NIM remains below its historical average.

NIM expansion will be driven by the same factors as last year. For starters, BOH will be able to improve a little on its funding costs. The bank was paying an average rate of 3.18% on its Term deposits last quarter, while renewal rates are well below 3%. Note that over 75% of the bank's CDs will reprice in the next six months.

Meanwhile, given how low BOH's securities yield is compared to prevailing market rates, repricing will continue to support its earning-asset yield. The spread between its roll-off and roll-on rates was over 230 basis points last quarter, and it will continue to be highly favorable this year. Plus, because BOH's NIM has continued to rise on a sequential basis, comps in the first part of 2026 will be soft for the firm. As such, readers can expect another strong year of margin-fueled growth.

Unlocking Buybacks

Aside from impacting its interest margin, BOH's investment securities have also had a big impact on its capital. Specifically, the unrealized losses within its AFS portfolio have weighed heavily on its ratio of tangible common equity to tangible assets. As BOH's bonds gradually reprice to prevailing rates, however, these losses are reversing. Alongside higher net income, this is helping to reflate BOH's capital cushion, with its TCE ratio expanding another 21 basis points last quarter.

As expected last time out, this process has reopened the door to share repurchases. BOH did $5 million in buybacks under its repurchase program last quarter. While a modest sum, it does mark a return after a lengthy absence. As BOH's profits and capital continue to improve, buybacks should become more meaningful in 2026 and beyond. For obvious reasons, this will be an extra tailwind to the bank's share-level earnings.

A Better Deal Than It Looks

The above has implications for BOH's valuation. At $72.67 as of this writing, the stock trades for 15.7 times 2025 EPS. While that doesn't look cheap, there are a couple of mitigating factors to consider. Firstly, a return to buybacks will naturally boost BOH's shareholder yield. Secondly, BOH still has plenty of recovery-driven growth in its tank. As I said, the bank will be facing some very easy comps this year, reflecting how depressed its NIM was early in 2025. Weighing that up, analysts expect BOH to earn $5.40 per share this year, rising to $6.14 by 2027. Relative to 2025, that maps to a compound annual growth rate of 15%. This strong growth will chip away at the stock's price-to-earnings ratio.

If we marry those points together, you can see that BOH is a better deal than it looks on paper. Not only is the "E" part of its P/E ratio growing at a healthy clip, but investors will be getting most of it returned to them via dividends and share repurchases. Historically, BOH has returned around 80% of its profits to shareholders. If we apply that rate to 2-year forward consensus EPS, investors will be looking at a ~7% shareholder yield. Top that up with GDP-like organic growth of 3%-4%, and investors retain a credible path to a double-digit annualized return. Accordingly, I am leaving my "Buy" rating for Bank of Hawaii in place.

data.title: The GLTR ETF Continues To Glitter In 2026
data.description:
Precious metals glittered in 2025 as prices of the four metals trading on the CME’s COMEX and NYMEX divisions posted extraordinary gains. Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) had been making new record highs for years after eclipsing $875 per ounce in 2008. After bullish key reversal formations in Q2 2025, silver (XAGUSD:CUR), platinum (XPTUSD:CUR), and palladium (XPDUSD:CUR) joined gold’s parabolic rally.

Trends are always a trader’s or investor’s best friend, and as the precious metals sector moved into 2026, it remained more than bullish.

The abrdn Physical Precious Metals Basket Shares ETF (GLTR) holds physical gold, silver, platinum, and palladium.

A bullish 2025 for the four leading precious metals

Gold, silver, platinum, and palladium are the four precious metals trading on the CME’s COMEX and NYMEX divisions.

The four metals posted extraordinary returns in the year that ended on December 31, 2025:

  • At $4,341.10, gold rose 64.37%.
  • At $70.603, silver rallied 141.44%.
  • At $2,034.50, platinum rose 127.57%.
  • At $1,651.40, palladium moved 81.51% to the upside.

Silver was the sector leader on a percentage basis, but all four precious metals posted explosive gains and moved into 2026 after gold, silver, and platinum futures reached record highs in December.

Gold and silver - New highs

Gold posted its ninth consecutive quarterly record high in Q4 2025.

The quarterly chart highlights the COMEX gold futures’ ascent that took the price to a record high of $4,584.00 per ounce in late December 2025. Gold closed 2025 just below its all-time peak. Gold rose to its 10th consecutive record high in early Q1 2026, surpassing $5,000 per ounce.

While gold exploded in 2025, silver’s rally was even more dramatic.

The quarterly chart highlights the COMEX silver futures’ incredible rally that took the price to a record high of $69.525 per ounce in late December 2025. Silver closed 2025 above $68.50, over $18 per ounce above the 1980 high. Silver first eclipsed the 1980 high in October 2025. In early Q1 2026, silver’s price continued its parabolic rally, rising to over $110 per ounce, more than double the 1980 peak.

Platinum and Palladium - Room to run

In 2025, platinum rose by more than 127%, second only to silver's explosive price action.

The quarterly platinum chart shows that platinum eclipsed its 2008 high in December 2025, rising to $2,128 per ounce. Platinum closed the year at above $2,000 and was closing in on the 2008 record high of $2,584.50 per ounce. Platinum broke out to the upside after trading the $1,000 per ounce pivot point for a decade. In Q1 2026, platinum futures reached $2,925 per ounce on January 26 as the bullish trend continued.

The quarterly palladium futures chart shows that the futures reached a high of $2,129 in December 2025 and closed above $1,650 per ounce at the end of the year. Palladium reached a record high of $3,425 in Q1 2022. Palladium continued to rise in January 2026, reaching $2,185.50 on January 26.

If gold and silver’s price action is a model, platinum and palladium could have room to run much higher in 2026.

GLTR owns physical precious metals

The fund summary for the GLTR ETF states:

On January 26, 2026, GLTR’s top holdings included:

At $274.69 per share, GLTR had $3.32 billion in assets under management. GLTR trades an average of just over 204,000 shares per day and charges a 0.60% management fee.

Given the four precious metals’ 2025 price performance, the latest Seeking Alpha ETF Grades awards GLTR the highest possible A+ grade in the momentum category. The 0.60% management fee earns a C- in the expense category, while the risk of the precious metals basket earns a C+. The assets and daily volume earn an A- grade in the liquidity category.

Seeking Alpha's overall Quant Ranking for GLTR is 27th out of 3,421 commodity-focused ETFs, or in the top 0.80% of the asset class.

Uncharted territory: The reasons to be bullish in 2026 and a reason for caution

GLTR glittered, no pun intended, in 2025 and early 2026.

The daily year-to-date chart highlights the 87.25% rise from $109.80 at the end of 2024 to $205.60 per share on December 31, 2025. GLTR reached a high of $275.91 on January 26, 2026, as the explosive price action in precious metals continued over the first month of 2026.

GLTR holds 100% of its assets in physical gold, silver, platinum, and palladium bullion, with the highest allocation to gold and silver. While the leading precious metals head into 2026 in bullish trends, the nearly 8% allocation to the far less liquid two platinum group metals could turbocharge the ETF, as platinum and palladium could have significant room to run higher over the coming months.

I continue to rate GLTR a Buy in early 2026, but prefer buying the ETF on price weakness, as this has been the optimal approach over the past years.

data.title: New York, Illinois Highlight State-Level Nuclear Support
data.description:
By Stacey Morris, CFA

Nuclear power has enjoyed unique bipartisan support from the federal government across the Trump and Biden Administrations. However, states are also focused on supporting nuclear development. The state-level emphasis on nuclear comes as states vie for economic opportunities, including hosting data centers, while leaders are also sensitive to upward pressure on utility bills. Notable news from New York and Illinois this month can help pave the way for the addition of new nuclear capacity in those states.

New York Raises Nuclear Target

In a speech on January 13, New York Governor Kathy Hochul announced a target of five gigawatts (GW) of new nuclear capacity for her state. This is up materially from a goal last summer to add one GW of nuclear power. The new target follows a robust response to the New York Power Authority’s recent solicitations of interest from communities and developers for advanced nuclear power development.

New York plans to create a new nuclear workforce development program to support this effort. Currently, New York has three reactors operating with a combined capacity of 3.4 GW. The nuclear plants operating in the state are owned by Constellation Energy (CEG).

Illinois Lifts Moratorium on New Large Reactors

Illinois also saw a notable policy development for nuclear power this month. Governor JB Pritzker signed the Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act, which lifts the state’s moratorium on new large reactors. The ban on new reactors had been in place since 1987. State legislation in 2023 had previously allowed for new small modular reactors (capacity of 300 megawatts or less) to begin construction in 2026.

Despite the ban of the last 36 years, Illinois leads the U.S. in nuclear power. The state has six nuclear plants encompassing 11 reactors, all owned by CEG. This includes the Clinton Clean Energy Center, from which Meta (META) will purchase power over a period of 20 years beginning in 2027 (read more). More than half of the state’s power comes from its nuclear fleet.

The new bill in Illinois paves the way for the potential addition of large reactors in the future. New reactors would most likely be the Westinghouse AP 1000. In October, the U.S. government partnered with Brookfield and Cameco (CCJ) to support the deployment of the Westinghouse reactors. The partnership is targeting at least $80 billion of new AP1000 reactors across the U.S. CCJ owns 49% of Westinghouse, while Brookfield owns 51%.

CCJ and CEG are currently the top two constituents of the VettaFi Nuclear Renaissance Index (NUKZX). NUKZX is the underlying index for the Range Nuclear Renaissance Index ETF (NUKZ).

For investors interested in the global growth opportunity for nuclear power, NUKZX includes a diversified group of companies across the nuclear value chain. Constituents include utilities like CEG, as well as companies focused on fuel, advanced reactors, and construction and services (read more).

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2026. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com

Original Post

data.title: DGRO Vs. The S&P 500: Balancing Dividend Income And Total Return In 2026
data.description:
Nearing the end of January, it is clear that volatility has returned in 2026. The current investing landscape is a confluence of unknowns, including: tariffs, supply chain shocks, inflationary pressures, debt concerns, AI bubble fears, and monetary policy uncertainty that conspire to test the fortitude of investors in the coming year. This does not foreshadow a bad year for stocks; rather, the market functions as a discounting mechanism. This means that current information is already priced into stocks, and future movement will depend on whether data surprises to the upside or the downside relative to investor expectations.

This environment offers aggressive investors ample opportunity to take risks in search of reward. One example is the monetary debasement trade: precious metals have offered handsome returns to start the year; however, an ample expectation of monetary debasement is already priced in. The factors listed above promise that prices will swing substantially in the coming year because the flow of coming information is high.

Some investors, particularly those in their distribution years, do not want to take large directional bets in their portfolios. For those seeking to navigate this environment without sleepless nights while minimizing uncertainty, I favor a “vanilla” approach.

The iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) remains my preferred vehicle to execute this strategy. The fund is not a flashy product attempting to beat the market with directional bets. Instead, it is diversified over hundreds of stocks with a preference for dividend-paying stocks, somewhat reducing exposure to technology (16.4%) in preference for financials (20.9%) and health care (17.4%). I first covered this fund in Sept. 2024, and since then it has returned 19.7% versus a 25.3% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY). However, DGRO has accomplished this with a beta of only 0.77. This means that the total return when adjusted for risk is essentially identical to the S&P 500, while providing investors with a 2.14% yield. This sustainable, growing yield does not sacrifice risk-adjusted return while insulating investors from the vicissitudes of the market.

The Quality Filter: Why DGRO's Dividend Growth is Sustainable

Investors should be cautious at balancing total return and dividend yield. Yield traps are common, particularly if investors sacrifice quality by chasing the highest-yielding stocks. The strength of DGRO is its refusal to compromise on a quality filter, which demands at least 5 years of uninterrupted dividend growth, a payout ratio below 75%, and yields not within the top 10% of the market. This avoids unsustainable dividends, without the need for active stock picking. As a passive fund, the expense ratio is only 0.08%, avoiding the cost of active management. The results speak for themselves, with the growth in share price closely matching dividend growth.

While past performance is no guarantee of future results, DGRO's performance has been excellent. Since inception (6/10/2014), the fund has returned 11.87% annualized, lagging the S&P 500 but closely mirroring the Morningstar US dividend growth index at 11.94%. While many ETFs yield more than DGRO, investors have sacrificed on total return. For example, some of the highest-yielding options below, like the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) and Vanguard's Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), are, while higher yielding, much more sensitive to interest rates and have trailed the market badly over the past 10 years.

Among dividend ETFs, DGRO falls in the lower category based on current yield, instead offering consistent growth and share-price appreciation. It is important to remember that yield does not matter to the extent that some believe. Dividends are priced into a stock or ETF, with the share price declining on the ex-dividend date to price in the payout. Further, investors can never escape Mr. Market, as the total return the market assigns will always be a major component of a stock or ETF's total return.

By prioritizing fundamental health metrics over raw yield, DGRO shifts focus from chasing yield to participating in the growth of profitable enterprises. In the face of 2026's macro-uncertainty, this approach nets “clean” exposure to equity growth without the interest rate vulnerabilities of higher-yielding, but potentially over-leveraged, high-yielders.

Sector Weighting: A Hedge Against Volatility and the Potential AI Bubble

The S&P 500 has become increasingly top-heavy in recent years, with the top 10 companies now comprising a record weighting within the index. While this concentration has provided massive gains, it also makes the S&P 500 increasingly a bet on the continuing growth of technology stocks and, by extension, AI. The top 10 stocks are all technology, save for Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) and currently compose 39.2%. In its history, the S&P 500 has never been so concentrated.

For investors wishing to steer clear of a directional bet on the continuing adoption of AI and diversify their holdings, DGRO offers a light exposure to technology at 16.4% versus the present 34.4% concentration of the broader market. In contrast, there is more exposure to financials and healthcare, while still having an overweight position in consumer staples and energy.

Being underweight technology also orients the portfolio toward a valuation-based approach. DGRO has a P/E ratio of 23.6x, significantly lower than the S&P 500 at 28.3x. With a beta of 0.77, DGRO is 23% less volatile than the S&P 500 with a 3yr standard deviation of 10.74%.

Conclusion

DGRO proves that boring can be beautiful. While the future is always uncertain, 2026 seems to present investors with a “confluence of unknowns,” testing the fortitude of even the most stalwart investors. While the starting yield is lower than some funds, I consider this a positive. As debate rages over the course of the Federal Reserve's independence, investors should be wary that the highest dividend payers are the most exposed to interest rate risk as bond proxies. This is a multifaceted risk because high-income stocks and funds tend to have lower growth, higher debt, and higher payout yield with respect to earnings. While the course of monetary policy may favor that, it must be noted that with higher-than-average uncertainty, higher-than-average changes in price are also possible.

By maintaining a lower tech weighting, a 0.77 beta, and a 2.14% SEC yield, DGRO serves as the perfect “vanilla” anchor for a dividend growth portfolio. It provides the growth necessary to outpace inflation, the earnings at a low payout ratio to provide distributions, and high quality, resulting in low volatility. For those seeking a growing income stream with lower risk and volatility compared to the broader market, DGRO is a compelling Buy.

data.title: U.S. Government Struggles To Keep A Lid On 10-Year Treasury Yield And Mortgage Rates
data.description:
The “rate check” on Friday to put a floor under the yen was the latest step.

On around midday Friday came the latest step, a “rate check,” with which Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent attempted to put a floor under the yen that had plunged against the dollar and push back down long-term US Treasury yields that had surged, as he saw the turmoil in the Japanese bond market and the plunge of the yen bleeding over into the US.

The New York Fed, at the request of the Treasury Department and acting as a fiscal agent for the Treasury Department, asked its primary dealers what exchange rate they would get if the NY Fed started buying yen through them. This “rate check” was a signal that the US government is ready to intervene in the currency market to support the yen against the USD.

As soon as this happened around midday Friday, the USD began to plunge against the yen, and the yen jumped from 159.2 yen to the USD to 155.7 by Friday evening (hourly chart via Investing.com):

On Wednesday, Bessent had blamed the surging long-term US Treasury yields on the bond-market meltdown in Japan, during which the 30-year Japanese Government Bond yield spiked by 42 basis points in two days and drove it to 3.91%, the highest since the 30-year bond was introduced in 1999. The crucial 10-year JGB yield surged by 15 basis points in two days. And the yen re-plunged against the dollar. The trigger had been Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s call for increased government spending with simultaneous tax cuts.

The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) had surged to 4.30% on Wednesday morning, up by 17 basis points in a week, even as the Trump administration is trying to get mortgage rates to come down. But mortgage rates track the 10-year Treasury yield, with a varying spread. And this surge of the 10-year yield caused mortgage rates to jump back to 6.20%, from 6.01% a few days earlier, according to the daily measure of 30-year fixed mortgage rates by Mortgage News Daily. And Bessent blamed that on the bond market meltdown in Japan.

“It’s very difficult to disaggregate the market reaction from what’s going on endogenously in Japan,” Bessent told Fox News at the time. And he said that he’d gotten in touch with Japanese officials, and said that he is “sure that they will begin saying the things that will calm the market down.”

With this two-step three-day jawboning — first on Wednesday when Bessent said he “got in touch” with Japanese authorities, and then on Friday, with the “rate check” — the 10-year yield dropped from 4.30% to 4.23% (hourly chart via Investing.com).

To help push mortgage rates down, the Government Sponsored Enterprises Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac started buying back some of the MBS they’d issued. This started in 2025. But on January 8, this was moved to the front pages when Trump directed Fannie and Freddie to buy back $200 billion in MBS, about the maximum they can buy back under current legal limitations.

But they don’t have the available cash to do that, they only have enough available cash to buy some MBS. They could also issue bonds and then use the cash proceeds to buy those MBS, but that would put further pressure on the bond market. So whatever.

This announcement was a masterful if temporary stroke of jawboning down mortgage rates, which plunged by 20 basis points combined on Friday, January 9, and Monday, January 12.

It didn’t last long, however. By Wednesday, January 21, mortgage rates were right back where they’d been on January 8. They’d just done a big U (daily chart via Mortgage News Daily).

Obviously, Bessent wouldn’t blame the surging long-term Treasury yields on the ballooning US deficit and the flood of new supply of bonds coming on the market that investors will have to absorb, and he wouldn’t blame it on inflation that accelerated further and that worries the bond market. Jawboning is a lot easier to do than to address those issues.

The bond market might not be happy for long with this jawboning. Inflation is a big issue for bond investors as bonds lose purchasing power due to inflation, and yield is supposed to compensate for the loss of this purchasing power, plus some. But long-term yields are too low to compensate investors for hotter inflation in the future.

And inflation keeps moving further away from the Fed’s target, amid government policies of prodigious deficit spending and Trump’s pressure on the Fed to cut short-term interest rates. They want to run the economy “hot,” meaning higher growth and more inflation, which provides fertile ground for inflation to bloom. Given this scenario, the bond market is still very sanguine, surprisingly sanguine, despite the recent ripples.
